coredata.dc:title: An amplified chemiluminescence system based on Si-doped carbon dots for detection of catecholamines
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Amjadi, Mohammad
Hallaj, Tooba
Manzoori, Jamshid L.
Shahbazsaghir, Tahmineh
coredata.dc:description: We report on a chemiluminescence (CL) system based on simultaneous enhancing effect of Si-doped carbon dots (Si-CDs) and cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB) on HCO3 −-H2O2 reaction. The possible CL mechanism is investigated and discussed. Excited-state Si-CDs was found to be the final emitting species, which are probably produced via electron and hole injection by oxy-radicals. The effect of several other heteroatom-doped CDs and undoped CDs was also investigated and compared with Si-CDs. Furthermore, it was found that catecholamines such as dopamine, adrenaline and noradrenaline remarkably diminish the CL intensity of Si-CD-HCO3 −-H2O2-CTAB system. By taking advantage of this fact, a sensitive probe was designed for determination of dopamine, adrenaline and noradrenaline with a limit of detection of 0.07, 0.60 and 0.01 μM, respectively. The method was applied to the determination of catecholamines in human plasma samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Chemiluminescence
Carbon nanomaterials
Si-doped carbon dots
Surfactant
Catecholamines

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by a grant from Iran National Science Foundation (INSF 95848790 ). Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of Si-CDs, (b) UV–visible absorption spectrum of Si-CDs, (c) Fluorescence spectra for Si-CDs excited at wavelengths of 300–420 nm, with increments of 20 nm.
Kinetic profile for H2O2-HCO3 − CL system in the absence and presence of Si-CDs and CTAB. Condition: HCO3 −, 0.5 M, H2O2, 0.075 M, CTAB 0.00075 M, Si-CDs 50 μL.

(a) CL spectrum of Si-CDs-H2O2-HCO3-CTAB CL system, obtained with continuous flow of reagents: Si-CDs, HCO3 − (1 M) and CTAB (0.0015 M) in one line and H2O2 (0.15 M) in other line. (b) Fluorescence spectrum of Si-CDs at λex = 420 nm.

The enhancing effect of various kinds of carbon luminescent nanomaterials on the H2O2-HCO3 − CL system in the presence and absence of CTAB. Nanomaterials include Si-CDs, citric acid-derived CDs (c-CDs), glucose-derived CDs (g-CDs), carbon nitride QDs (CN dots), phosphor-doped CDs (P-CDs) and sulfur and nitrogen co-doped CDs (S,N-CDs). An amount of CTAB and each nanomaterial, which give the optimum response, were used in experiments. Conditions: HCO3 −, 0.5 M, H2O2, 0.075 M.
Possible mechanism of the CL system.
Effect of radical scavenger on Si-CDs-CTAB-H2O2-HCO3 − CL system.
Analytical performance of the proposed CL system for the determination of catecholamines.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: ∆I = I0 − I, I0 and I are the relative CL intensity in the absence and presence of analyte, respectively, and C is the concentration of analyte.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The relative standard deviation (RSD) was obtained for five replicate determinations of 0.5, 0.1 and 5.0 μM of dopamine, noradrenaline and adrenaline, respectively.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Tolerance limit of interferences on the determination of noradrenaline (0.1 μM).

row.entry: K+, Na+, Albumin, Zn2+, Mg2+, Ca2+,PO4 3−, Cl−, NO3 −, glycine, sucrose, alanine, lactose, glucose, cysteine

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Tolerance limit of interferences on the determination of dopamine (0.5 μM).

row.entry: K+, Na+, albumin, Zn2+, Mg2+, Ca2+,PO4 3−, Cl−, NO3 −, glycine, sucrose, alanine, lactose, glucose, cysteine

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Determination of catecholamines in human plasma samples.

head.ce:title: An amplified chemiluminescence system based on Si-doped carbon dots for detection of catecholamines

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Tabriz, Tabriz 5166616471, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We report on a chemiluminescence (CL) system based on simultaneous enhancing effect of Si-doped carbon dots (Si-CDs) and cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB) on HCO3 −-H2O2 reaction. The possible CL mechanism is investigated and discussed. Excited-state Si-CDs was found to be the final emitting species, which are probably produced via electron and hole injection by oxy-radicals. The effect of several other heteroatom-doped CDs and undoped CDs was also investigated and compared with Si-CDs. Furthermore, it was found that catecholamines such as dopamine, adrenaline and noradrenaline remarkably diminish the CL intensity of Si-CD-HCO3 −-H2O2-CTAB system. By taking advantage of this fact, a sensitive probe was designed for determination of dopamine, adrenaline and noradrenaline with a limit of detection of 0.07, 0.60 and 0.01 μM, respectively. The method was applied to the determination of catecholamines in human plasma samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon-based nanofluorophores such as carbon dots (CDs) [1,2], graphene quantum dots [3], carbon nitride quantum dots [4] and so on are being widely used in various chemical fields. Intense research efforts have focused on improving and controlling the physicochemical properties of carbon nanomaterials. Doping CDs with heteroatoms modifies their electronic structure, and is an effective way to tune their intrinsic properties [5–8]. Such doped CDs have found applications in the diverse fields involving bio-imaging, sensing, catalysis and photoelectronic transforming [9,10].

One of sensing techniques in which doped CDs can be applied is chemiluminescence (CL) [11–16]. The use of CDs in the weak traditional CL reaction can be useful for improving their efficiency as well as extending their applications. However, we still far from realization of full advantages of doped CDs in this field. In the present work, we investigate the synergic effect of Si-CDs and a cationic surfactant (cetyltrimethylammonium bromide, CTAB) on HCO3 −-H2O2 ultra-weak CL reaction. We show that because of this effect, CL intensity of system is remarkably enhanced. Therefore, an amplified CL system is introduced.

By using this CL reaction, a new probe was developed for detection of some catecholamines in biological samples. Dopamine, noradrenaline and adrenaline, which belong to catecholamines, are neurotransmitters and/or hormones in the peripheral and central nervous system. They are involved in several physiological mechanisms and are related to some of the most prevalent human pathologies, such neurological disorders as Parkinson's disease, Alzheimer, schizophrenia and hyperactivity. Therefore, they can be applied as clinically relevant biomarkers for specific disease states or monitoring treatment efficacy. Currently, many researchers are interested in the function and measurement of catecholamine neurotransmitter and several analytical methods have been developed for their determination [17–24]. Most of these methods involve separation of catecholamines by HPLC and electrophoresis and subsequent detection by spectroscopic or electrochemical techniques. We demonstrate that Si-CD based CL system can be applied for detection of catecholamine neurotransmitters as a direct methodology. However, it may also be used as a sensitive and simple detection technique (without need for derivatization) for separation methods.

The CL signals were recorded by LUMAT LB 9507 chemiluminometer (Berthold; www.berthold.com). Fluorescence and CL spectra were obtained by RF-540 spectrofluorimeter (Shimadzu, Japan). UV–vis spectra were recorded on a Cary-100 Spectrophotometer (Varian, Australia). The size and shape of Si-CDs were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Leo 906, Zeiss, Germany). The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum was obtained on a FT-IR spectrophotometer (Tensor 27, Bruker). EDS spectrum was obtained by TeScan-Mira III instrument (Czech Republic). A thick layer of Si-CDs was coated on a carbon grid to avoid the interference of substrate peak.

All reagents were of analytical reagent grade. Ultra-pure water obtained from aqua MAX - Ultra 370 water purification system (Young Lin instrument Co., Korea) was used for all experiments. Citric acid monohydrate, 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES), Cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB), H2O2, NaHCO3, NaOH were all purchased from Merck (Darmstadt, Germany). Human plasma samples were acquired from Blood Transfusion Center (Tabriz, Iran).

Si-CDs were synthesized by a hydrothermal method according to the literature [25]. The homogenous mixture of 1.0 mL APTES, 1.0 g citric acid and 15 mL deionized water was prepared and transferred into a Teflon equipped stainless steel autoclave, and heated at 180 °C for 4 h in a muffle furnace. After cooling down the autoclave to ambient temperature, pale yellow Si-CD solution was obtained.
Detailed procedures for synthesis of other carbon nanomaterials are given in Electronic Supplementary Material (ESM).

For CL measurements, an amount of 50 μL of Si-CDs and 500 μL of HCO3 − (1.0 M) were added into a CL cuvette. Then an appropriate volume of sample or standard solution of catecholamines was added and the volume was adjusted to 925 μL with ultra-pure water. At last, 75 μL of 1.0 M H2O2 was automatically injected into the cuvette. Simultaneously, the CL signal was recorded versus time and the maximum value of CL intensity was taken as the analytical signal.

In order to prepare spiked human plasma samples, appropriate volumes of dopamine -or noradrenaline standard solution were added to 500 μL of plasma in a centrifuge tube. To remove the proteins, 2.0 mL of 0.1 M Ba(OH)2 and 1.8 mL 0.1 M ZnSO4 were also added into the tube, then the obtained mixture was centrifuged for 15 min. Subsequently, supernatant solution was diluted to 5.0 mL with water and a proper aliquot was analyzed according to the general procedure.

The size and morphology of Si-CDs were studied by TEM. Fig. 1a indicates that Si-CDs have spherical shape and their diameters are in the range of 4 ± 2 nm. As can be seen in the FT-IR spectrum (Fig. S1, ESM), the stretching vibration of SiO appears at about 1130 cm−1. The peak at about 1410 cm−1 can be attributed to CC vibration and the peak at 1700 cm−1 to stretching vibration of CO. The peaks at about 1355 and 2945 cm−1 are assigned to bending and stretching vibrations of CH, respectively. Additionally, the broad bands at 3200 and 3448 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibrations of OH and NH, respectively. EDS spectrum (Fig. S2, ESM) indicates that the carbon, oxygen and silicon content of Si-CDs are about 45, 45.9 and 9.2 w/w%, respectively.

Fig. 1 shows the UV–vis absorption and florescence spectra of Si-CDs under different excitation wavelengths. UV–vis absorption spectrum exhibits two peaks ascribed to π–π* transition and n–π* transition at about 220 and 330 nm (Fig. 1b), respectively. Fluorescence spectra of Si-CDs indicate an excitation wavelength-dependent fluorescence feature (Fig. 1c). The maximum fluorescence emission at 440 nm is obtained when the excitation wavelength is 340 nm.

The influence of as-prepared Si-CDs on the H2O2–HCO3 − CL reaction was investigated. According to Fig. 2 , the kinetic profile of this reaction in the absence of Si-CDs exhibits two peaks. The first peak appears 1 s after injection of the oxidant solution and the second peak after 15 s. It is known that (1O2)2* and (CO2)2* are two major emitting species of H2O2–HCO3 − reaction, which are responsible for these peaks, respectively [26,27].

However, in the presence of a small amount of Si-CDs, only one peak appears after 20 s from injection of the oxidant solution, whose intensity is about 15-fold of initial CL reaction. It can be assumed that Si-CDs react with the oxy-radicals generated during HCO3 −–H2O2 reaction, which leads to the electron and hole injection and formation of Si-CDs+ and Si-CDs−. Then, Si-CDs* are produced from annihilation of Si-CDs+ and Si-CDs−, which return to the ground state by photon emission. This process competes with the formation of (1O2)2* and (CO2)2*, leading to the diminishing of their profiles and appearance of only one peak [26,28,29]. The summary of proposed mechanism is illustrated in Scheme 1 .

In the next step, the effect of CTAB was examined on this reaction and found that the CL intensity in the presence of CTAB is enhanced by about 70-fold and its appearance time shifts to 10 s. CTAB micellar structures formed on Si-CDs surfaces may attract and concentrate reactive oxy-radicals (OH and O2 −) and facilitate the electron and hole injection to Si-CDs, leading to an increase in the formation of Si-CDs* [30,31]. It also can protect excited-state Si-CDs and decrease their non-radiative deactivation. It should be mentioned that the effect of CTAB was also investigated on the HCO3 −–H2O2 CL reaction and observed that the CL signal of this reaction was intensified just about 4-fold with addition of CTAB.
The CL spectrum of reaction (Fig. 3a) exhibits a peak at 500 nm and is similar to the fluorescence spectrum of Si-CDs, confirming that the emitting species in this system is excited-state Si-CDs.

To prove the critical role of free radicals in the introduced CL system, the influence of thiourea, DMSO [32], NaN3 [33] and quercetin [34] as well-known radical scavengers for OH, 1O2 and O2
−, respectively, on the CL intensity of the system was studied. As can be seen in Table 1 , all these radical scavengers indicated a notable negative effect on the CL intensity.

We also investigated the effect of components used in the preparation of Si-CDs (citric acid and APTES) on the CL intensity of H2O2-HCO3 −-CTAB system. It was observed that these components or their mixture do not contribute in the enhancement effect.

It is interesting to compare these results with those obtained by using other kinds of CDs. For this purpose, we prepared undoped CDs with two different sources i.e. citric acid (c-CDs) and glucose (g-CDs), as well as sulfur and nitrogen co-doped CDs (S,N-CDs), phosphor-doped CDs (P-CDs) and carbon nitride quantum dots (CN dots) (See ESM for details of synthesis procedures). Fig. 4 shows the effect of these nanomaterials on H2O2–HCO3 − reaction, in the absence and presence of CTAB. In the absence of CTAB, S,N-CDs show the strongest enhancing effect, which is probably due to their higher fluorescence quantum yield (82%). However, the enhancement effect of CTAB on the Si-CDs-H2O2-HCO3 − CL reaction was much more remarkable than its effect on other systems. This is probably due to a special interaction between Si-CDs and CTAB. It is known that surfactants form hemi-micelles and admicelles on the surface of SiO2 particles [35]. Since Si-CDs have somewhat similar structure, CTAB may form such micellar structures on their surface, leading to stabilization of excited-state Si-CDs and decreasing non-radiative deactivation.
We investigated the effect of concentration of reagents on the CL intensity to establish the optimum conditions for the introduced CL system.

The influence of HCO3 − concentration was studied in the range of 0.1–0.8 mM. As can be seen from Fig. S3a (ESM), there is a linear relationship between the CL intensity and HCO3 − concentration. Due to the decrease in the solubility of HCO3 − at high concentration, 0.5 M HCO3 − was chosen as the optimum concentration for further work.

The effect of H2O2 concentration on the CL intensity was studied in the range of 0.025 to 0.3 M. As shown in Fig. S3b (ESM), the CL intensity reaches a maximum at 0.075 M. Therefore, this amount was chosen for further examination.

We examined the influence of three kinds of surfactants including cationic (CTAB), anionic (sodium dodecyl sulfate, SDS), and nonionic surfactants (Triton X-100), on the Si-CDs-H2O2-HCO3 − system. The results indicated that CL intensity is significantly enhanced in the presence of CTAB while, no remarkable variation in CL signal was observed with addition of SDS and Triton X-100. According to Fig. S3c (ESM), the highest CL intensity was achieved at 0.75 mM CTAB concentration. The enhancement effect of CTAB on the CL system may be attributed to the fact that anion radicals can be attracted and concentrated on the positively charged micelles or hemi-micelles formed on Si-CDs surfaces [30,31]. As a result, they have more reaction opportunities. Furthermore, as mentioned before, micellar structures protect the excited-state Si-CDs and decrease their non-radiative deactivation, which leads to a significant increase in the CL intensity.

Finally, examining the effect of Si-CD amount on the system indicated that the CL signal is intensified with increasing the amount of Si-CDs up to 50 μL, and then decreases at higher values (Fig. S3d, ESM). This decrease may be due to self-quenching effect of Si-CDs at high concentrations.

For evaluation of analytical applicability of Si-CDs-H2O2-HCO3 −-CTAB CL reaction, the influence of catecholamines such as dopamine, adrenaline and noradrenaline was investigated on this system. We observed that the CL intensity diminishes in presence of these compounds, even at trace levels. This is probably because of competition of catecholamines with Si-CDs for oxidant. Based on this fact, a new probe was established for the determination of these compounds, and their analytical figure of merits was obtained (Table 2 ). The results indicate that the developed CL probe has good linearity, relatively high sensitivity and suitable precision. Comparison between this method and some other reported CL methods for the determination of dopamine and noradrenaline is shown in Table S1 (ESM). Selectivity of the method was studied by examining the effect of some common species found in biological samples. The tolerable concentration ratios for interferences in relative error of 5% are given in Tables 3 and 4
. The obtained results show that the method has a good selectivity for the determination of dopamine and noradrenaline in biological samples. Mutual interference of catecholamines were also studied and shown in Tables. As can be seen, noradrenaline can tolerate 10-fold concentration of other catecholamines while dopamine can tolerate 20-fold of adrenaline and 5-fold of noradrenaline. If concentrations of interferences exceed these tolerance limits, separation techniques such as HPLC may be used before CL detection.

The method was exploited to determine dopamine and noradrenaline in human plasma samples. The results are reported in Table 5 . Statistical analysis of these results indicates that there are no significant differences between added and found values, which confirm the accuracy of established method.

In this study, the enhancing effect of Si-CDs and CTAB was investigated on the ultra-weak H2O2-HCO3 − CL reaction and as a result, an amplified CL system was introduced. The study on the CL mechanism indicated that Si-CDs* are emitting species, which are mainly generated by electron and hole injection. It was also found that due to concentrating the reactive oxy-radicals and protecting excited-state Si-CDs, CTAB remarkably amplifies the CL signal. Moreover, based on the diminishing effect of catecholamines on the Si-CDs-H2O2-HCO3 −-CTAB CL system, a simple and sensitive probe was developed for determination of dopamine and noradrenaline in biological samples. It should be emphasized that the applicability of this CL system is not limited to these particular analytes and it has potentiality for other analytical applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by a grant from Iran National Science Foundation (INSF 95848790).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2018.04.058.

New development in carbon quantum dots technical applications
Fluorescent graphene-like carbon nitrides: synthesis, properties and applications

Improving the functionality of carbon nanodots: doping and surface functionalization

Silicon-hybrid carbon dots strongly enhance the chemiluminescence of luminol
Silicon carbon nanoparticles-based chemiluminescence probe for hydroxyl radical in PM 2.5
Application of the chemiluminescence system composed of silicon-doped carbon dots, iron(II) and K2S2O8 to the determination of norfloxacin
Sulfur and nitrogen co-doped carbon quantum dots as the chemiluminescence probe for detection of Cu2+ ions
Chemiluminescence of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots for the determination of thiourea and tannic acid

Analytical methods for determining urinary catecholamines in healthy subjects
Catecholamines and methods for their identification and quantitation in biological tissues and fluids
Determination of catecholamines by flow-injection analysis and high-performance liquid chromatography with chemiluminescence detection

Quantum dot-enhanced chemiluminescence detection for simultaneous determination of dopamine and epinephrine by capillary electrophoresis

Chemiluminescence arising from the decomposition of peroxymonocarbonate and enhanced by CdTe quantum dots
Determination of ammonia in water based on chemiluminescence resonance energy transfer between peroxymonocarbonate and branched NaYF 4:Yb3+/Er3+ nanoparticles
A novel chemiluminescence method for determination of bisphenol abased on the carbon dot-enhanced HCO3 −–H2O2 system
Flow-injection analysis of hydrogen peroxide based on carbon nanospheres catalyzed hydrogen carbonate–hydrogen peroxide chemiluminescent reaction
Micelle modified-carbon nanosphere enhanced chemiluminescence from reactive oxygen species for the detection of hydrogen peroxide

Radical cations in the OH-radical-induced oxidation of thiourea and tetramethylthiourea in aqueous solution
